title: AI Content Is Everywhere on Social Media, Especially LinkedIn (4 minute read)
content: AI-generated content is spreading across social media, with longform posts hit the hardest. Pangram data from over 1M posts shows 13.8% flagged as AI, rising to 25% for posts over 250 words. LinkedIn drives nearly two-thirds of flagged content, with over 40% of longform posts fully AI-generated. X shows the highest combined AI use when including mixed content. Reddit replies remain mostly human, but top-level posts show higher AI rates, revealing gaps in moderation.

title: OpenAI Appears to Be Missing Its Sales Goals by a Vast Margin (2 minute read)
content: OpenAI's $100 billion ad-revenue target for 2030 requires three things to happen at once. Advertisers moving decades of search and social budgets into chatbots, OpenAI out-competing Google and Meta for that spend, and the entire AI ad market growing roughly 100 times from where it sits today. The category's entire addressable market currently tops out around $5.4B, and OpenAI itself is on pace to miss its own ad revenue forecast by about 90% this year.

title: Copywriting Breakdown: How to sell something ordinary (4 minute read)
content: Strong marketing often comes from better research instead of better copy. Claude Hopkins won customers in a crowded category by asking hundreds of consumers what they wanted, measuring how Palmolive performed against those needs, and turning the results into specific proof points. The ad replaced vague claims with numbers such as 250 times more lather, 15% water absorption in one minute, and lather that lasted 10 minutes. Research, measurable evidence, and clear messaging can make an ordinary product stand out even when competitors offer similar features.

title: Don't block personal emails (1 minute read)
content: Blocking personal emails in B2B forms can hurt conversion rates. Instead, detect domains like Gmail or iCloud and require a company URL field. With name and company, you can still find or enrich work emails. This keeps friction low while preserving lead quality. Many high-intent leads still submit personal emails, often due to autofill behavior.

title: Swag That Works: The New Rules for Trade Show Giveaways (5 minute read)
content: 47% of marketers already treat branded merchandise as a core marketing channel, and 87% of recipients keep and use what they receive rather than tossing it. Middesk built a tiered swag strategy around that shift at Money20/20, gating higher-value items behind booth actions like booking a sales meeting or agreeing to a case study, then generated 10 times its activation spend in new pipeline and 26 times more in influenced pipeline. The gifting also produced a surprise: a steep jump in LinkedIn followers driven entirely by pins and luggage tags that were never meant to be the campaign's main goal.

title: How the founder of Morning Brew built a Claude content machine that never runs out of ideas (43 minute video)
content: Alex Lieberman's Content Machine scans seven days of Slack, Notion, Gmail, and meeting notes, plus the accounts he follows on X and LinkedIn, then ranks 15 content ideas a day so the blank page never becomes the bottleneck. Six AI personas modeled on interviewers like Tim Ferriss and Howard Stern question him until they extract real specifics, and his voice-to-texted answers trace every sentence back to something he actually said. Before anything posts, a six-persona Writer's Council scores the draft and blocks it from going below a 9 out of 10.

title: Opal U: Become a certified AI expert in 5 days (3 minute read)
content: Opal U is a free 5-day live cohort for senior marketers who need to prove AI ROI and move fast. Built for VPs and heads of marketing who feel stuck in the hype and need practical output. Spend one hour a day building three AI agents that automate real work and save 10+ hours a week.

title: Why Your Top Keywords Are Quietly Losing Volume (11 minute read)
content: Head term queries are what fund Google's ad business, since two-word category terms draw 5x to 15x the CPC of longtail equivalents, and those same head terms are now shrinking in 7 of 9 consumer categories. Entertainment's head term volume fell 16% year over year, the sharpest decline in the dataset, in a category where AI tools already give users a credible substitute for a Google search entirely. The bigger risk for Google isn't cannibalized clicks on searches that still happen, it's users skipping the search box altogether for tools like ChatGPT on exactly the head term queries the ad auction depends on.

title: How much creative debt are you carrying? (6 minute read)
content: Creative work needs recurring time on the calendar or it will always lose to urgent tasks. "Creative debt" is the cost of repeatedly delaying creative projects until avoiding them becomes part of your identity instead of a temporary choice. Treat creative projects like recurring commitments by scheduling dedicated time, reduce project scope, and keep momentum through small, consistent actions instead of waiting for the perfect moment. You should also remove pressure to prove ROI so creative work can exist without immediate business value. The goal is to stop delaying meaningful projects before postponement blocks future creative output.

title: How I (accidentally?) went from Lifecycle Marketer to Product Manager (5 minute read)
content: A lifecycle marketer transitioned into a product manager role through embedded product work, not a planned switch. Leading iterative in-app projects and using AI to prototype solutions shifted ownership from execution to problem definition. Internal support helped formalize the move. The role change requires giving up solo execution and focusing on defining problems and aligning teams. Lifecycle skills translate well, especially prioritization and building user experiences.

title: 10 Creative Ways to Write with AI Without Losing Your Soul (15 minute read)
content: Tips on how Ahrefs uses AI to create content, plus the experiments they've been running.

title: AI SEO Tip: Microsoft Clarity Topic Insights (1 minute read)
content: Microsoft Clarity Topic Insights is a free AI visibility tool that shows how often your content is cited in AI-generated answers, how much it influences those answers, and which competitors are being cited.

title: How Michelob Ultra and Leo Messi Prepared for the Most Unpredictable Marketing Moment of the World Cup (3 minute read)
content: Michelob Ultra built its Messi tribute ad around 20 years of career accomplishments, letting the same creative work whether Argentina won or lost the final.

title: Branded search is becoming a less reliable proxy for brand demand (3 minute read)
content: Branded search has long been a proxy for brand demand, but it's becoming less reliable. Data shows branded queries dropping even as demand holds steady, pointing to a measurement gap, not a demand decline. AI search is shifting decisions upstream, answering questions before users search for brands. The risk is misreading weaker search as weaker brands and underinvesting in brand building. Branded search still matters, but it should be one signal, not the source of truth.

title: Does topical authority matter in AI Search? (7 minute read)
content: Topical authority still matters in AI search, with data from Semrush's analysis of 50,000+ brands showing AI answers develop category owners over time. In ChatGPT's June 2026 data, only 15.2% of categories had a clear owner, leaving 89.3% of AI search demand in categories without one. Category owners also showed stronger brand search volume, organic traffic, and authority. Build depth in core topics rather than chase broad content coverage.

title: Thoughtless features never add up to a thoughtful product (4 minute read)
content: Teams ship “ham biscuit” features fast, solving surface problems but missing real user needs. Idea generators stay detached, copying competitors or AI outputs, while execution follows without question. The result is bloated products that hurt clarity and sales. Better products come from defining real workflows, not stacking features. Focus on the job to be done, validate outcomes, and design end-to-end experiences instead of isolated fixes.

title: What everyone gets wrong about category creation (6 minute read)
content: Category creation only works when it reflects a real job to be done instead of a new marketing label. Companies create confusion when they invent platform names that don't match a clear customer responsibility or workflow. It's better to create a new job to be done before naming a category, sell products as a suite when buyers purchase them separately, or lead with the product the market already understands and expand from there.

title: SubjectLine.com (Tool)
content: This is a free email subject line tester that scores over 15M subject lines and helps improve email performance. Free accounts provide subject line history, score tracking, and AI-powered rewrites. Pro accounts ($9.99/year) add open rate predictions, industry benchmarks, and project organization tools.

title: How to check Chrome's Accessibility Tree (1 minute read)
content: AI agents don't view websites like humans do. They rely on the Accessibility Tree, a machine-readable version of your page structure. Check yours in Chrome DevTools to see how agents interpret your content. Clean up headings, labels, links, and alt text to make your site easier for AI systems to understand and surface.

title: RCS or SMS? It Depends on the Task (3 minute read)
content: RCS now reaches 96% of US devices and offers rich media, interactivity, and branding, but costs at least twice as much as SMS. Like HTML email, it adds capability, not necessity. Use RCS when visuals or actions help complete a task like browsing products or resolving issues. Stick with SMS for simple, urgent updates like order confirmations or codes.

title: AI Is Working Overtime. So Am I (10 minute read)
content: AI users save an average of 5.1 hours a week but lose 3.3 hours learning or troubleshooting the same tools. 77% report a heavier workload, while 23% report AI-related anxiety. The shortfall comes from generation time being mistaken for completion time, since a report that takes one minute to draft still needs its research checked and its logic fixed before it is usable. The technology accelerates the first draft while leaving the harder work, review and accountability, exactly where it was.

title: My goal is to never apply for a marketing job ever again (7 minute read)
content: Build a personal brand before you need it by staying visible and optimizing LinkedIn.

title: Tell your engineer to change "Loading..." to "Thinking..." (2 minute read)
content: Products that hide their "thinking" step read as less warm to users.

title: The biggest technical SEO time-wasters to avoid (6 minute read)
content: Chasing minor SEO fixes like redirects, crawl budget, 404s, or tool flags wastes time when they don't impact rankings or user experience.

title: Is the Age of Reading Actually Over? (5 minute read)
content: Nearly 400 independent bookstores opened in 2025, and print book sales are higher than a decade ago. Readers also spend more time with books daily than they did twenty years ago. Claims that reading is dying dismiss this growth as niche while treating declining attention spans as proof of collapse. Yet people still consume more words across digital formats. The shift is intentionality. Long-form reading is becoming a deliberate choice, raising the bar for creators to earn sustained attention.

title: Content Is No Longer Enough (5 minute read)
content: As content becomes more saturated, brands are focusing less on producing more and more on building communities. Dentsu, NFL Mexico, and Warner Bros. Discovery argue that fandom and belonging create stronger connections than reach alone. Audiences want to participate, not just consume, making creators and fan communities valuable partners. The opportunity for brands is to build spaces where people can engage with shared interests beyond the content itself.

title: +39% conversions by removing a prominent call-to-action (2 minute read)
content: Removing a prominent homepage CTA increased conversions by 39% because it guided first-time visitors through the service before asking them to request a quote. The original CTA sent people straight to a form that asked technical questions many could not answer, so many left before converting. The updated journey introduced service details, trust signals, and expert credentials before visitors reached a quote CTA on the relevant service page. Match each CTA to the visitor's level of understanding so the next step feels simple and appropriate instead of asking for a commitment too early.

title: Stop Marketing to Strangers (3 minute read)
content: Most marketing over-focuses on acquiring new audiences while neglecting people who already know you. With declining external traffic, visitors now arrive with context, yet brands still treat them like strangers. The real opportunity is “step-two” marketing, guiding engaged users toward clear next action and deeper content. Growth comes from improving experience, not just reach. Small execution gaps like broken promises or poor follow-through quietly erode retention over time.

title: Use AI to Find Your Fastest Route to Cultural Traction (Webinar)
content: Attention today goes to the brands that are part of the conversation, not the ones interrupting them. In this webinar, Vanina Schick will walk through how to connect your brand into POVs with strong cultural energy and use AI to find where you can enter cultural conversations online. It will take place on July 23 at 9 AM PT.

title: The 2026 Paid Playbook: Audience, Channels, and AI (12 minute read)
content: AI made producing ads nearly free without creating any new attention to fill, so more ads now chase the same finite audience and simply raise the price of being seen. That moves the advantage to the audience layer, which a competitor cannot reverse-engineer the way it can copy creative or messaging. Resolving buyer identity before uploading lists opens the cheaper broad-reach channels that most B2B teams avoid, and running holdout experiments alongside attribution reveals which channels actually drive incremental conversions.

title: Netflix: The Battle for Attention (5 minute read)
content: Netflix spent years dismantling scheduled television, and now it's rebuilding pieces of the same bundle it once disrupted. Live sports, third-party channels, and short-form video are being layered in to turn the app into a daily habit rather than a destination for a single hit show. The tentpole approach, a handful of NFL and MLB games instead of full season packages, produced six of Netflix's 10 largest sign-up days over five years despite consuming just over 5% of content spending. If those additions do not feel cohesive, Netflix risks becoming the bloated bundle it once replaced.

title: The $400,000 Chief of Staff Is the CEO's Secret Weapon in the AI Age (5 minute read)
content: AI-era CEOs are increasingly hiring chiefs of staff as strategic partners, not just assistants. The role has grown as leaders face greater complexity, with chiefs of staff helping execute initiatives and build workflows. While AI may automate some administrative tasks, skills like judgment, communication, and influence remain difficult to replace. The position is also becoming a leadership pipeline, with many chiefs of staff moving into executive roles.

title: Zara's ‘death pants' are trending because people keep tripping on them (4 minute read)
content: Zara's wide-leg pants became “death trousers” after viral injury videos turned a design flaw into a social media trend.

title: Netflix says live events bolster ads but affect viewer engagement (2 minute read)
content: Netflix is using live events to win subscribers and grow ad revenue even though they generate fewer total viewing hours than on-demand content.

title: England vs The Internet: What did Reddit make of the night it all fell apart against Argentina? (6 minute read)
content: Redditor sentiment often evolves through debate and social proof instead of a single viral opinion.

title: Audiences Don't Want More Footage. They Want In (3 minute read)
content: Studios respond to fan demand with more content, but audiences are looking for participation, not passive viewing. Fans raised on games and creator culture remix, extend, and interact with story worlds. Data shows deeper fans want new ways into a franchise, not just more of the same. Companion formats like podcasts keep attention but lack agency. The real gap is participatory infrastructure, and the next phase of entertainment will let fans actively shape worlds between releases.

title: The Shift from SEO to Social Content (4 minute read)
content: Search intent isn't disappearing, it's being intercepted before it reaches a company's website. AI overviews answer the query directly inside Google, and GenAI assistants like ChatGPT and Perplexity now guide most B2B buyers through research and shortlisting without a single visit to a vendor's blog. That interception is why SEO leads that once arrived weekly now arrive monthly, and why the fix has to happen on the social platforms and communities where B2B buyers now spend that research time instead. The way through is repurposing what SEO already built, turning the case studies, reports, and articles into video, carousel, and thought leadership formats.

title: I can't believe some marketing teams skip this (5 minute read)
content: Customer census surveys are an underused marketing motion that helps teams collect customer insights and build a stronger evidence library. The best surveys are cross-functional, tightly scoped to around 10-15 questions, and targeted to active users for higher-quality responses. Treat the send like a campaign with follow-ups and incentives, then move quickly on responses to turn feedback into testimonials, reviews, and advocacy opportunities.

title: TV Ads for B2B - why it works and where to start (5 minute read)
content: TV advertising is becoming a viable growth channel for B2B as digital ads get more expensive and saturated. Connected TV lets companies retarget existing audiences with 15 to 30 second storytelling, often using repurposed content like founder videos or customer testimonials. Budgets can start small, around $50 per day, and performance improves once traffic scales. TV also lifts other channels, with reported gains like a 25% increase in branded search. Test early and treat TV as a long-term, compounding channel.

title: Sauce (Tool)
content: Sauce helps teams measure and prove how off-site and creator-led content drives AI visibility and search performance. It shows which posts appear in engines like ChatGPT and Perplexity, who created them, and for which queries. A 7-day free trial is available.

title: Build a Hermes inbox triager in 90 mins (10 minute read)
content: This guide walks through building a Gmail triage agent with Hermes and the read-only IMAP tool Himalaya. The agent sorts incoming mail into five labeled folders, scores each by urgency, and pings Slack only when something crosses a genuinely urgent threshold, staying silent otherwise.

title: Anthropic Will Pay Its New Standards Editor $300K to Argue About Commas (3 minute read)
content: Anthropic is hiring a Standards Editor for around $300K to oversee grammar, tone, and clarity across its communications. The role highlights a broader trend as AI makes content generation cheaper and human judgment more valuable. While models can produce endless drafts, companies still need editors who can protect brand voice, simplify complex ideas, and decide what is worth publishing. The hiring trend suggests editorial judgment is becoming a premium skill in an AI-driven content market.

title: When AI chatbots boost vs hurt sales (3 minute read)
content: AI chatbots work best when shoppers need help comparing products or sorting through large assortments. Across four experiments with 1,339 people, chatbot recommendations increased purchase intention by 44.7% for products that are hard to evaluate, such as apparel that varies by fit, size, and style. Chatbots also increased engagement for simple products that have many options, but reduced purchase intention for simple products that have small assortments because they added unnecessary friction. Use chatbots where they simplify complex decisions and organize choices instead of adding another step to an easy purchase.

title: DJI used GoPro to kill GoPro (3 minute read)
content: DJI used GoPro's brand to make consumer drones easier to buy, then replaced GoPro by building the complete product itself. The GoPro mount gave DJI product validation and customer demand without camera R&D or category education. DJI walked away after GoPro asked for two-thirds of the profits because it believed flight technology created a stronger competitive advantage than cameras. DJI then launched four new drone generations over 30 months while GoPro built a competing product, which let it stay years ahead before GoPro's Karma drone failed and left the market.

title: Technical SEO Tip: Use Brave to get your content indexed by Claude faster (1 minute read)
content: Use Brave's "Submit URL" tool to get content indexed faster by Anthropic/Claude.

title: How Polaroid Is Winning Gen Z by Rejecting AI and Screens (4 minute read)
content: Polaroid recodes film's slow development and lack of editing as premium benefits, mindfulness, authenticity, pitting one unrepeatable print against an iPhone's vault of near-identical shots.

title: The 9 GA4 events you need to track for ecommerce (1 minute read)
content: Most sites only track ‘view_item', ‘add_to_cart', and ‘purchase', but you should also track ‘view_item_list', ‘select_item', ‘view_cart', ‘begin_checkout', ‘add_shipping_info', and ‘add_payment_info'.

title: The Open Web Is Shifting From An Audience Economy To A Context Economy (5 minute read)
content: The next phase of ad tech is shifting from knowing who someone is to understanding what's happening around them right now. Instead of relying on fixed audience segments built from past behavior, new bidding infrastructure lets ad auctions read signals like timing and nearby activity in real time. Containerizing that decision-making inside the auction itself cuts response times by roughly 80%, giving algorithms enough time to weigh context instead of just matching an ID to a segment. Advertising stops chasing who people are and starts responding to the moment they're actually in.

title: Anthropic's newest ad is creeping people out (3 minute read)
content: Anthropic's new ad, “There's hope in hard questions”, is getting backlash for its dark tone and imagery. It opens with a burning house and cuts to scenes of surveillance, homelessness, mining labor, and graveyards while asking questions about AI trust and control. The campaign mirrors Anthropic's usual positioning as the ethical alternative in AI, but many viewers say it feels heavy-handed.

title: How to use content pillars to build your social strategy (7 minute read)
content: The best-performing content pillar systems in 2026 work as concentric circles instead of a flat list of themes. A narrow core pillar builds algorithmic and audience authority faster than a broad one, while adjacent pillars share that same audience and add new angles without diluting it. A pillar only counts as adjacent if it still reinforces why someone followed the account in the first place, since anything broader just makes the feed generic.

title: How to measure the impact of AI search the right way (8 minute read)
content: AI search is driving real demand, but most teams still measure it through referral traffic, which rarely shows up. Only around 1% of users click AI citations, and much of that traffic is misattributed or shows up as direct, making last-click models unreliable. Instead, use an “AI visibility ladder” that tracks leading indicators like crawl rate and citation share. It should also look at quality signals like how a brand is positioned in answers, and lagging indicators like self-reported attribution and win rate.

title: SEO & AI Tip: In AI Overviews, being recommended is the goal, not being cited (2 minute read)
content: AI Overviews recommend brands based on commercial prompts, not informational content. Ranking for informational queries rarely pays off downstream in commercial visibility. Build content around high-value commercial queries and support each page with pricing, comparison, methodology, and brand information that helps AI justify recommending your business.

title: Allan (Tool)
content: Allan is an AI email platform for DTC brands that builds a different email for each recipient instead of sending one campaign to everyone. It selects subject lines, copy, and creative based on user behavior, and runs continuous tests across every send to optimize performance. A 30-day free trial is available.

title: Brandom: Why Fandom is the Future of AI Search (52 minute video)
content: AI search is moving away from brand-owned content toward third-party voices on Reddit, YouTube, and LinkedIn. LLMs tend to trust sources that sit further from brand control, which makes customer, employee, and community advocacy more important than blog content. Reddit is emerging as a key offsite channel, similar to what blogs were for SEO, but requires real participation instead of spam. The shift is also blurring lines across SEO, PR, and community, while measurement relies on signals like share of voice and citations.

title: A Subtle Google Ads Bidding Change (2 minute read)
content: Campaign targets now matter more than past overperformance, so review every target-based bidding campaign before Google changes bidding on August 17. Campaigns that currently exceed their target ROAS may lose efficiency because Google will optimize toward the set goal instead of continuing to outperform it. Raise targets in small steps when recent performance is more than 20% above the current goal, or set a higher custom target if stronger performance is realistic. Advertisers that care more about conversion volume than efficiency should consider switching to a maximize bidding strategy.

title: Could EA's new advertising platform open the floodgates for in-game ads? (3 minute read)
content: Six years after apologizing for intrusive ads in UFC 4 and disabling them, EA is betting that real-time, targeted placements can succeed where static pop-ups failed. The new platform is built to enhance gameplay rather than interrupt it, giving brands the ability to adjust messaging in real time and measure impact for the first time. However, most gaming ad spend still lives on mobile, where casual play tolerates interruption more easily. Console and PC audiences treat games as a more focused experience that ads have historically struggled to enter without friction.

title: Building the nation's largest soccer league (7 minute read)
content: Community creates stronger long-term growth than paid acquisition when the customer experience gives people a reason to return and invite others. The Footy League built scalable operations behind its community, including customer service, registration systems, sponsor-ready media, and local experiences that support national expansion without losing its identity. It uses paid marketing to attract first-time players, but newsletter marketing and word of mouth create higher retention and stronger referrals. Brand comes from the customer experience instead of visual identity, so values need to appear in everyday interactions such as onboarding, training, and support.

title: OpenAI's ChatGPT ads could miss $100 billion revenue target (1 minute read)
content: OpenAI's forecast assumes it will capture search ad budgets at scale and outperform every ad format in history.

title: AEO Play: Ramp's Help Center (1 minute read)
content: Ramp creates robust pages on its Help Center documentation, which is often cited by AI search in the B2B space.

title: OpenAI's first device will reportedly be a ‘humanlike' rechargeable speaker (2 minute read)
content: OpenAI is building a screenless AI companion speaker that uses ChatGPT capabilities to interact naturally with users.

title: How a 180-year-old news institution prepares for its next reinvention (20 minute read)
content: The Associated Press is turning 180 years of journalism into a data business, deconstructing its reporting into structured and unstructured data sold to other industries alongside its traditional news distribution. The shift runs alongside a deliberately short AI licensing strategy, one-to-three-year deals built on protecting journalists' intellectual property and securing fair value as models train on AP content. Newspapers, once AP's dominant customer base, now generate under 10% of its revenue, with data licensing and diversified income filling the rest.

title: Gen Z's ‘Borrowed Nostalgia' Is Reshaping Brand Marketing (2 minute read)
content: Growing up with instant access to decades of archived content is compressing the nostalgia cycle for Gen Z, who now form emotional attachments to eras that predate their birth at a far higher rate than older generations. Two-thirds of Gen Z report this "borrowed nostalgia", compared with Gen X's tilt toward the 80s and millennials' toward the 90s and 2000s. Music ranks as the strongest trigger across generations, which is why campaigns like Gap's Katseye ad, built around Kelis's 2003 hit "Milkshake", land with both the generation that remembers the song and the one discovering it fresh.

title: Advertising works differently for Millennials and Generation Z (3 minute read)
content: Millennials show higher advertising recall than Generation Z across nearly every channel, digital and traditional alike, from a 30-point gap on television, 56% vs. 26%, to smaller but consistent gaps on social media, search, and streaming video. The exception is video games, where recall is nearly identical between the two generations. Gen Z's lower recall tracks lower overall media consumption, but the generation is more responsive to influencer-driven purchases than Millennials, 39% vs. 34%.

title: Lifecycle marketing vs. lead lifecycle (9 minute read)
content: B2B teams often confuse lifecycle marketing with lead lifecycle. Lifecycle marketing drives activation, retention, and expansion, while lead lifecycle manages pipeline stages. The two work together through pre-sale nurture, closed-lost reactivation, expansion campaigns, and lead quality feedback loops. Sales-assisted PLG teams can also use signals like usage limits, feature requests, and ICP fit to trigger sales handoffs.

title: How do I measure the pipeline contribution of paid media? (3 minute read)
content: Connect paid media to the CRM before launching campaigns so every optimization ties back to pipeline instead of platform metrics. Use multi-touch attribution, causal modeling, and incrementality testing together because each method fills gaps the others miss and gives a more reliable view of channel performance. Report results in business metrics such as contribution margin, LTV to CAC, payback period, cost per first meeting, and cost per pipeline dollar because those numbers align marketing with finance. Companies that optimized for pipeline instead of clicks cut cost per MQL by 27%, increased conversion rate by 18%, and improved ROAS by 138%.

title: Nail Profit before Site Extras, Owner Says (3 minute read)
content: Focus first on profitability before adding advanced features to your e-commerce website. Reduce returns, improve customer service, and build a shipping model that works before investing in AI tools or extra apps. Design every product and collection page as a landing page because visitors often arrive from ads, social posts, or other links instead of the home page. A 15 to 30 second product video can increase conversions, and fast-loading MP4 videos with clear audio create a better brand experience than GIFs or poor-quality video.

title: 400+ Sites Analyzed: 5 Data-Backed Traits to Thrive in Google & AEO with Cyrus Shepard (Webinar)
content: Cyrus Shepard, founder of Zyppy SEO, analyzed 400+ sites to find 5 traits driving organic growth and 17 content types that still perform in a zero-click world. This webinar will explain how to rethink AI's role by cutting low-impact output and focusing on assets that earn visibility in Google and AI search. It will take place on July 23 at 3 PM ET.

title: Cart Culture: The Data Retailers Want Before You Walk In the Door (Webinar)
content: This webinar breaks down what retailers expect before listing a product, focusing on data, not gut feel. It covers how to bridge the gap between DTC traction and in-store readiness and how to build a credible data story months before pitching to improve your chances of landing shelf space. It takes place today at 2:30 PM ET.

title: What a Former TikTok Shop Manager Learned From $14M in Live Sales (6 minute read)
content: TikTok Shop expert Jason Termechi analyzed $14M+ in live sales and found most brands go live before building the foundation needed to convert. He recommends establishing TikTok Shop sales history first, then optimizing broadcasts with metrics like GPM, tap-through rate, watch time, and click-to-order rate. His playbook includes flash sales, weeks of pre-promotion, rotating hosts, and real-time adjustments to improve engagement and sales.

title: Why search ROAS depends on paid social more than you think (6 minute read)
content: Search ROAS often looks strong only because paid social is running alongside it, generating brand awareness that later surfaces as branded search queries search then gets credited for capturing. Last-click attribution can't see social impressions that never converted directly, creating a structural bias where search inherits demand it didn't create. The effect is delayed by four to eight weeks, so teams that cut social based on isolated ROAS comparisons often see search CPAs climb months later without connecting the two. Geo holdout tests and brand search volume tracking are the more reliable way to measure the actual cross-channel effect.

title: Why marketing conferences are still worth it (5 minute read)
content: Conferences feel costly and exhausting, but they accelerate relationships and opportunities faster than remote work.

title: 27 Things That Actually Get a SaaS to $10K MRR (3 minute read)
content: Pick a tiny niche, talk to users before building, and ship fast, ugly versions early to get real feedback and traction before wasting time on guesses.

title: X is teaching its AI algorithm something social networks once understood (2 minute read)
content: X is now weighting mutual followers more heavily in replies and recommendations, after finding its ranking algorithm wasn't using relationship data.

title: Every AI Marketing Platform Now Sells a “Brand Voice” Feature. They're All Selling the Same Voice (5 minute read)
content: AI marketing platforms like Jasper standardize “brand voice” by applying generic tone guidelines to content that's already interchangeable, producing average output at scale. This creates a feedback loop of sameness. The issue isn't the tools, it's the input. Simple checks like swap, output, and reconstruction tests reveal weak voice. Brands grounded in clear beliefs and real decisions avoid being flattened.

title: Over 200 economists warn that more AI job losses are coming: “We must act now” (2 minute read)
content: More than 200 economists, including 16 Nobel Laureates, have warned that AI could accelerate job displacement and reshape the economy faster than previous industrial shifts. A new open letter urges policymakers and tech leaders to create safeguards that ensure AI complements workers rather than replaces them. The warning comes as companies announce AI-related layoffs, with 99% of executives expecting AI to reduce headcount in some capacity within two years.

title: Where AI agents get stuck on your site (5 minute read)
content: AI agents don't struggle with your site, they struggle with your pricing. When it's hidden, hard to parse, or blocked, they default to third-party sources. Even published pricing often fails if it's buried in JavaScript, calculators, or messy layouts. Make pricing explicit, centralized, in plain text, and crawlable. Add structure and reduce friction.

title: You're losing press coverage (2 minute read)
content: Press coverage can be lost when reporters can't quickly access the assets they need. Make it easy to cover a company by creating a public press kit with downloadable logos, screenshots, brand assets, and copy-ready stats. Avoid gated folders or hard-to-find pages, since journalists will move to easier sources. Adding a dedicated press page with clear URLs and frequently used metrics helps reporters quickly find and cite information.

title: Pages that Print (Website)
content: A curated gallery of landing pages that generate verified revenue. It highlights what drives conversions, helping users avoid copying designs that don't perform. The platform also offers teardowns and diagnostics to identify what's hurting a page and how to fix it.

title: How to Know If LinkedIn Paid Will Generate Better Leads (Webinar)
content: LinkedIn Paid can be incredibly effective, but only when the right foundations are already in place. Lauren Loreto will walk through how to evaluate whether your business or brand is actually ready for paid visibility and what to focus on first before spending budget. The free webinar will take place on July 15 at 9 AM PT.

title: An inside look at Mutiny's growth engine (15 minute read)
content: Mutiny built its growth engine by combining rapid testing and focused channel investments instead of spreading effort across many initiatives. It improved conversions by 71% after testing a more specific homepage headline, then paired broad awareness tactics with account-based outbound that focused on a small list of high-value prospects. It used borrowed audiences and ecosystem partnerships to expand reach while tracking performance across every stage of the funnel. The team treated every experiment as a way to find repeatable growth, then doubled down on the channels and tactics that produced results.

title: Empathy and delight mean nothing when the software is disrespectful (6 minute read)
content: Empathy and delight haven't improved software because respect is missing. Products remove user control and push work onto users through rigid flows and forms. This starts with designers who overestimate their role and scales through systems that prioritize efficiency over agency. LLMs extend this by defaulting to doing tasks for users, generating low-value output that increases review burden and rewards volume over judgment. The result is bloated features and worse experiences. Respectful software gives users control, keeps useful friction, and communicates clearly.

title: This K-Beauty Brand Is Collapsing The Marketing Funnel To Grow Its US Customer Base (4 minute read)
content: Sulwhasoo is collapsing the funnel by pairing CTV and DOOH with retargeting.

title: EU Commission preliminarily finds Meta apps addictive (2 minute read)
content: Meta may face fines and forced product changes in Europe after the EU ruled that Facebook and Instagram's addictive features likely violate its laws.

title: The 4 S's of YouTube Success (5 minute read)
content: YouTube growth is attributed to subscribers, suggested, search, and shorts.

title: Millions of Americans take on debt and drain savings to afford food (3 minute read)
content: About 20% of working-age adults used long-term savings to pay for groceries in the past year.

title: The Tech Bro-ification of Marketing (4 minute read)
content: Marketing is getting a tech makeover with titles like “narrative engineer” to make the same work sound higher status. Around 70% of marketers are women, and as fields skew female, pay and status tend to drop, so companies borrow engineering language to boost credibility and shift who it attracts. AI is pushing tech to recognize storytelling and taste, but instead of calling it marketing, they relabel it. This keeps the idea that work only feels important when it sounds technical.

title: In an era of ‘baddies,' companies are racing to keep up with the influencer economy (3 minute read)
content: Brands are shifting toward influencer-style marketing built around real customers and employees instead of traditional creators. Companies like ResMed are hosting events, running “baddie” community pages, and encouraging everyday users to create content that feels organic. The strategy reflects declining trust in traditional ads and stronger influence from peer-like content, with 40% of Gen Z favoring influencer recommendations. These efforts can drive engagement and normalize niche products, but they're still highly orchestrated and don't always work.

title: How Atlan Turned 52 Pages Into a 35% Share of AI Citations (9 minute read)
content: Atlan won a 35% share of AI vendor citations by building 52 high-quality pages that answer complete buyer questions instead of chasing individual keywords. It focused on comparison pages, buyer guides, and decision content that include expert authors, fresh updates, cited research, proprietary data, case studies, and balanced product assessments that AI models can trust. The same pages also perform well in search because 71% of its 3,886 ranking US keywords trigger AI Overviews, which lets one page earn both search traffic and AI citations. Strong content quality matters more than publishing volume because well-structured pages can earn AI citations even when organic rankings are not at the top of search results.

title: What Al Pacino can teach you about copy (5 minute read)
content: Strong persuasion follows a clear pattern. Start quietly to pull attention instead of pushing for it. Build trust with honest admissions that make claims more credible. Anchor everything to one core idea and make it concrete with specific details people can picture. Frame the decision as a clear choice where doing nothing has a cost. Then let the audience reach the conclusion themselves, which increases buy-in.

title: ChatGPT Search & Fan-Outs Capture (Chrome Extension)
content: ChatGPT Search & Fan-Outs Capture helps you see exactly what searches ChatGPT performs behind the 4scenes to generate responses. It's an advanced Chrome Extension designed to capture, analyze, and export SearchGPT conversations. It differentiates between Search (traditional queries), Shopping (shorter queries using SearchApi.io to scrape Google Shopping), and Images fan-outs.

title: Building an AI-Native Email Marketing System (6 minute read)
content: Running email inside Claude replaces fragmented workflows with a single system that connects your ESP via API, analyzes performance, generates campaigns, and improves over time. The setup uses Claude with API connectors, claude.md for brand context, custom email skills, scheduled audits, and Claude Design for templates. Data flows into live dashboards built with Claude Code widgets, while competitor emails are tracked via Gmail connectors and ReallyGoodEmails scraping. All tests and learnings are stored in a shared GitHub repo, creating a continuous loop where campaigns and iterations compound automatically.

title: Why frontloading your ad spend usually backfires (7 minute read)
content: Spending big at launch rarely speeds up growth and often backfires. Early campaigns lack data, so costs are highest and optimization is limited. Frontloading budget can inflate CPCs, delay insights, and hurt confidence when results miss. Instead, start small, find what works, then scale.

title: Your AI Chatbot Just Made My Cheeseburger Order Worse (5 minute read)
content: Context switching is becoming one of the biggest drivers of bad AI-driven customer experience. An airport restaurant's QR ordering system asked a customer to switch WiFi networks, create an account, and check topping boxes before a frustrated server stepped in and took the order in under a minute. People still prefer human support to AI overall, though that gap narrows in lower-risk categories like retail and travel where AI already outperforms. The fix isn't more automation, it's building AI that's judged by the same speed and ease standard as the human it replaces.

title: Disney+ is considering a free streaming tier (1 minute read)
content: Free streaming made up 18.7% of US TV watch time in April.

title: The Devil Wears Prada 2 Marketing Breakdown (3 minute read)
content: The campaign treated the movie like a living fashion event instead of a film release.

title: Scannable product tags vs. none (1 minute read)
content: Adding clickable product tags beat standard product cards by making Klaviyo's multi-product offering clearer and faster to scan.
